slides.title: Exploring hybrid environments to scale quantum-safe networks
Beyond QKD Applications of near-term Quantum Networks

polls.text: Have you read the entire (or large chunks of) the multiplane-arch draft?

chat.author: Rodney Van Meter
chat.text: We still have the view of the chair, not the speaker...

chat.author: Rodney Van Meter
chat.text: @meetecho how do we move "speaker camera" to the speaker instead of the chair?

chat.author: Rodney Van Meter
chat.text: Thanks!

chat.author: Rodney Van Meter
chat.text: Hi @angela, Wojtek reordered since you were late.

chat.author: Melchior Aelmans
chat.text: Interesting presentation and congrats on your first one at the IETF!

chat.author: Rodney Van Meter
chat.text: @dave Plonka would love to hear more about that meeting going on in...Oklahoma, did you say?

chat.author: Rodney Van Meter
chat.text: There is also a lot of education-oriented work going on.

chat.author: Melchior Aelmans
chat.text: You might want to consider leveraging space for longer range quantum communications. Less loss then in fiber hence you can span over  100km

chat.author: Rodney Van Meter
chat.text:
Also, <advertising hat on> @dave see RFCs 9340 and 9583, and
https://arxiv.org/abs/2311.02367

chat.author: Rodney Van Meter
chat.text:
And https://www.youtube.com/@QuantumCommEdu/courses
<advertising hat off>

chat.author: Dave Plonka
chat.text: Tnx, Rod. will also share with my REN colleagues.

chat.author: Shane Kerr
chat.text: Annoying that I have to learn about quantum position verification, which I never heard of but seems like cool magic. :wink:

chat.author: Junxiao Shi
chat.text: While I don't understand most of  draft-irtf-qirg-qi-multiplane-arch-02, Section 5.3.4. Quantum QoS Parameters is very nice reference for simulator designers.

chat.author: Rodney Van Meter
chat.text: Position verification is cool, but has caveats.

chat.author: Rodney Van Meter
chat.text: See the work of Harry Buhrman

chat.author: Dave Plonka
chat.text:
Aside (re: the earlier quantum network applications and R&E netoworks: here is the U.S. meeting I mentioned at the mic:
https://www.greatplains.net/2026/04/30/quantum-partnership-workshop-at-surf/

chat.author: Dave Plonka
chat.text: (performance accessing it from IETF network currently seems terrible. YMMV.)

chat.author: Rodney Van Meter
chat.text:
Re: standardization, there will be a day-long workshop on standards for both quantum computing and quantum communications at IEEE Quantum Week in Toronto in September.
See
https://qce.quantum.ieee.org/2026/qce26-schedule/workshop-schedule/
WKS::QPOLY::QSYS::31 - Quantum Sciences Standards – International Status

chat.author: Melchior Aelmans
chat.text: thanks for sharing Rod, this is interesting

chat.author: Rodney Van Meter
chat.text: Maybe we should be doing standards...?

chat.author: Wojciech Kozlowski
chat.text: What would we standardise though?

chat.author: Melchior Aelmans
chat.text: a research group cannot really do standards ;)

chat.author: Wojciech Kozlowski
chat.text: *assuming a wg is established or elsewhere

chat.author: Melchior Aelmans
chat.text: in that case there could be some things to standardize yes

chat.author: Wojciech Kozlowski
chat.text: components I guess?

chat.author: Angela Cacciapuoti
chat.text: components are still IMO immature, but it would be very interesting starting from the terminology, guess?

chat.author: Rodney Van Meter
chat.text:
Obviously there is interest in standards. We would have to recharter (or spawn) QIRG-->QIWG.
Note I'm not actually suggesting this yet, but we should stay aware of this other work and keep it as an active thought, when this work moves from research-oriented drafts toward working-network, interoperability-focused RFCs.

chat.author: Rodney Van Meter
chat.text: Everything we are currently doing in QIRG is comfortably IRTF work.

chat.author: Rodney Van Meter
chat.text: The other standards bodies don't seem to have as clear a two-track approach as IETF<-->IRTF, so I think some of what is being done in those other orgs is closer to IRTF-like than IETF-like.

chat.author: Melchior Aelmans
chat.text: Agree rechartering would be an option in the future. Terminology would be an interesting starting point. It has been tried in different fora already but never really succeeded imho.

chat.author: Dave Plonka
chat.text: Aside, re: Rod's comment... in the example, using the IPv6 documentation prefix (2001::db8::) would be nice (instead of RFC1918.)

chat.author: Dave Plonka
chat.text: 2001:db8:: (typo'ed before)

chat.author: Angela Cacciapuoti
chat.text: we could be the right forum (at least I hope) since we have also technical depth in the quantum domain and not only in the classical one. My impression is that in other fora this is not always the case

chat.author: Rodney Van Meter
chat.text: @Melchior I think working on terminology is good, and converging on it is incredibly valuable, but it's hard to impose top-down, it needs to be a real working-person's consensus.

chat.author: Rodney Van Meter
chat.text: Moreover, it doesn't need to be standards, I think an RG is fine.
